cards.title: Special 1: Staging Loaded
cards.cardSummary: Best tested Board 1 route. Load early chests, stage near tiles 24–30, then cross and cash post-wall red upgrades.
cards.profile: Special Board 1 is a wall-staging board. The old immediate-cross model underplayed it badly. Board 1 works because early chests are already value, and staging near the wall lets you convert that payload into a much stronger post-wall upgrade payoff.
notes: Board 1 is not very hand-size sensitive after staging; staging behavior matters more than +1 card.
Hand 4: EV 87.19 / rainbow 0.556 / wall 48.3%. Hand 5: EV 93.71 / rainbow 0.593 / wall 45.4%. Hand 6: EV 91.31 / rainbow 0.553 / wall 42.0%.
Aim to set up near tiles 24–30 before committing to the wall.
Early chests are already owned payload; staging does not erase that value.
Wall-success tests around 168 EV with about 1.25 rainbow average.
If the wall fails, Board 1 still has early chest value, unlike Board 2.
Owned upgrade cards highest-tier-prioritize, then randomly pick within that tier.
cards.useWhen: Use as the default Special Board 1 plan for realistic starts. Load chest bodies, stage near the wall, then cross.
cards.avoidWhen: Avoid blind crossing from too far back. The old no-staging route under-crossed and tested far lower.

walkthrough.heading: Load
steps: Collect early chests as upgradeable bodies.
Do not judge green/blue chests only by current value; they can become upgraded payoff slots.
Take life and movement when they improve staging or wall access.

walkthrough.heading: Stage
steps: Treat tiles 24–30 as the wall-staging band.
Do not ask only ‘can I cross now?’
Get into a better wall position before committing if the payload is loaded.

walkthrough.heading: Cross
steps: Once staged, use the wall solver to reach tile 37+.
Crossing unlocks the red-upgrade zone.
If the wall succeeds, Board 1 becomes a very premium special board.

walkthrough.heading: Cash
steps: Use red upgrades on owned chests after the wall.
Upgrade cards target the highest eligible owned rarity first, random within that tier.
Post-wall upgrades are the reason staging is worth it.

perStartingHand.note: Board 1 is not very hand-size sensitive after staging; staging behavior matters more than +1 card.

cards.title: Special 1: Rainbow Wall
cards.cardSummary: Older immediate-cross route kept for comparison. It under-crosses Board 1 compared to Staging Loaded.
cards.profile: This was the first Board 1 route, but staging tests showed it leaves major value on the table. Use Special 1: Staging Loaded as the default.
notes: Chests first, trap wall second, red upgrades last.
Are the owned chest slots good enough to justify crossing the wall?
Calculates actual hand route to tile 37+, trap hits, and life required.
Red upgrades can target already-collected chests on this board.
Softly protects the final correction card; it does not forbid spending it.
Upgrade cards highest-tier-prioritize, then randomly pick inside that tier.
Legacy comparison only; default is now Special1_Staging_Loaded.
cards.useWhen: Use when you have several owned chest slots, enough life/card reach to cross, and red upgrades still available after the wall.
cards.avoidWhen: Avoid crossing when the hand cannot reach past the wall, life cannot pay the trap toll, or the red upgrade payoff is weak.

walkthrough.heading: Chest zone
steps: Collect enough chest bodies to make later red upgrades matter.
Do not dismiss green/blue automatically; they can become rainbow slots.
Take movement and life tiles when they improve the future wall solve.

walkthrough.heading: Wall check
steps: Solve the actual current hand to tile 37+.
Compare expected owned-upgrade gain against trap/life cost.
Cross only if the score clears the strategy threshold.

walkthrough.heading: After wall
steps: Use red upgrades on owned chests.
Upgrade cards target the highest eligible owned rarity tier, then random within that tier.
Keep routing simple; do not over-chase empty pathing.

cards.title: Special 1: Safer Wall
cards.cardSummary: Conservative older wall route. Use only as a comparison; staging is now the recommended Board 1 plan.
cards.profile: A stricter version of Rainbow Wall for runs where you want fewer bad crosses.
notes: Requires 6 owned chests, 5 upgrades ahead, and +1 life buffer.
Fewer crosses, fewer disasters, but may miss some profitable aggressive routes.
Legacy comparison only; default is now Special1_Staging_Loaded.
cards.useWhen: Use when the wall route looks sketchy or you want a safer recommendation.
cards.avoidWhen: Avoid when you need a faster cross and are willing to take a thinner wall line.

cards.title: Special 2: Bank & Cross
cards.cardSummary: Default for realistic 4–6 card Board 2 starts. Bank red upgrades, preserve enough access, then cross decisively.
cards.profile: Special Board 2 is wall-access-gated and starting-hand sensitive. With realistic 4–6 starting cards, Bank & Cross is the default. Unlike Board 1, staging does not freely improve EV because every delay can weaken the upgrade/access balance. The wall is still the run: cross it and the board has premium payoff; fail it and chest value can be near-zero.
notes: Red upgrades first, trap wall second, chest zone last.
Board 2 is meaningfully hand-size sensitive; 6 cards is much healthier than 4–5, while 7+ is a rare/high-setup case.
Hand 4: EV 46.61 / rainbow 0.296 / wall 75.4%. Hand 5: EV 50.60 / rainbow 0.328 / wall 76.8%. Hand 6: EV 61.23 / rainbow 0.408 / wall 83.0%.
How many red upgrades can you bank before the wall without bricking movement?
Uses deterministic wall solve when available, but can use survival crossing after enough upgrades/life are banked.
Banked red upgrade cards are used on future chest tiles in the late chest zone.
Banked red upgrades target the highest eligible future chest rarity tier, random within that tier.
Corrected targeting test: strong value route, but Aggro Cross narrowly ranked higher by average value.
4–6 starting cards are the realistic baseline. In this band, Bank & Cross is the default.
7+ cards sharply improves Board 2 EV, but those starts are rare or confusion-card dependent.
The wall is the run. Wall success tests near ~67–70 EV; wall failure can be near-zero.
Movement cards are access, red upgrades are payoff. Keep enough movement to cross; do not let extra movement crowd out red upgrades.
Board 1 can stage freely because early chests are already owned value. Board 2 must preserve upgrade payload and access.
Life-first staging improved wall success but did not beat Bank/ForceCross EV in 4–6 starts.
cards.useWhen: Use for normal Board 2 starts with 4–6 cards.
cards.avoidWhen: Avoid treating this as guaranteed premium if your wall access is weak. For rare 7+ card/high-access starts, compare Aggro Cross.

walkthrough.heading: Starting hand check
steps: 4–6 cards: play Bank & Cross as the default.
7+ cards or strong confusion setup: Board 2 becomes much more premium; Aggro Cross may compete.
Do not evaluate Board 2 EV without noting starting hand size.

walkthrough.heading: Upgrade zone
steps: Bank red upgrades as payoff.
Keep movement only when it solves upgrade access, wall access, or late chest-zone access.
Remember every extra movement card competes with a red upgrade card.

walkthrough.heading: Wall
steps: Prioritize wall success. The trap wall is not guaranteed.
If you cross, the board acts like a premium special board.
If you fail, the early zone may produce little or no chest value.

walkthrough.heading: Chest zone
steps: Spend banked red upgrades into late chests.
Upgrade cards target highest eligible rarity first, random within that tier.
After wall access is solved, payoff comes from converting chest-zone targets.

perStartingHand.note: Board 2 is meaningfully hand-size sensitive; 6 cards is much healthier than 4–5, while 7+ is a rare/high-setup case.

cards.title: Special 2: Chest Zone Fill
cards.cardSummary: Crosses earlier and fills more chest slots. Lower value ceiling, better 7-chest rate in testing.
cards.profile: A completion-oriented Board 2 route. It sacrifices some red-upgrade banking to reach and fill the late chest zone more often.
notes: Best 7-chest rate among the Board 2 tested strategies.
Safer wall/completion style, but lower EV because it sacrifices payoff.
Hand 4: EV 35.39 / rainbow 0.172 / wall 77.0%. Hand 5: EV 48.63 / rainbow 0.260 / wall 89.4%. Hand 6: EV 52.10 / rainbow 0.276 / wall 94.6%.
Lower average value and lower rainbow rate than Bank & Cross.
Good when chest count matters more than upgrade value.
This route accepts lower upgrade banking to reach/fill the chest zone more often.
cards.useWhen: Use when you want more 7-chest completions on Board 2.
cards.avoidWhen: Avoid when you want the highest expected value.

perStartingHand.note: Safer wall/completion style, but lower EV because it sacrifices payoff.

cards.title: Special 2: Aggro Cross
cards.cardSummary: Aggressive Board 2 variant for rare 7+ card, high-access, or confusion-card starts. Not baseline default.
cards.profile: Aggro Cross improves when you enter Board 2 with unusually strong hand size/access. In realistic 4–6 starts, Bank & Cross is slightly better. At 7–8 starting cards, Board 2 EV rises sharply and Aggro can compete or edge ahead.
notes: Lower upgrade banking requirement than Bank & Cross.
Close to Bank at 4–6, but Bank wins the realistic baseline; Aggro is mainly for high-hand/high-access starts.
Hand 4: EV 45.42 / rainbow 0.294 / wall 67.6%. Hand 5: EV 50.10 / rainbow 0.334 / wall 70.6%. Hand 6: EV 60.20 / rainbow 0.404 / wall 77.8%.
Faster access to chests, slightly lower average value.
Good when the wall package is strong but upgrade-zone routing is poor.
Corrected targeting test: ranked #1 by average value in the 2,000-run Board 2 retest.
Every stored movement card competes with a red upgrade card. Movement is access, not payoff.
Keep enough movement to cross/reach chests; do not hoard weak or extra movement if red upgrades are available.
Heavy hand-pressure penalties underperformed. The current default keeps hand pressure as guidance rather than a hard dump rule.
Not the default for realistic 4–6 starts.
Use this more seriously with 7+ starting cards, strong access, or confusion-card setup.
cards.useWhen: Use as the current default Board 2 route when you want the highest corrected-test value.
cards.avoidWhen: Avoid when safe red upgrade tiles are still easy to collect.

walkthrough.heading: Upgrade zone
steps: Prioritize red upgrades as the payoff cards.
Take movement only when it solves access to more upgrades, the wall, or the chest zone.
Do not let weak movement cards crowd out red upgrade cards.

walkthrough.heading: Wall
steps: Cross once you have enough upgrade payoff and enough life/access.
Board 2 does not want infinite banking; hand pressure can make earlier crossing correct.
The wall behavior itself is the same as Board 1.

walkthrough.heading: Chest zone
steps: Cash banked red upgrades on the late chest zone.
Upgrade cards target the highest eligible chest tier first, random inside that tier.
If hand pressure was managed well, most stored cards should now be payoff cards, not dead access.

perStartingHand.note: Close to Bank at 4–6, but Bank wins the realistic baseline; Aggro is mainly for high-hand/high-access starts.

cards.title: Special 3: Rainbow/Red High Ceiling
cards.cardSummary: Highest tested EV Board 3 route. Skip low bands, preserve chest slots, and push for red/rainbow bands.
cards.profile: Special Board 3 is not a wall board. It is a slot-preservation board. Rarity bands climb as you move forward, so the main loss condition is filling your 7 chest slots too early with green, blue, or mediocre purple chests. The high-ceiling route accepts lower 7-chest completion in exchange for far better average value.
notes: Preserve slots for the late bands.
Board 3 is highly hand-size sensitive; extra cards greatly improve access to late red/rainbow bands.
Hand 4: EV 118.96 / median 39.0 / 7-chest 40.1% / rainbow 0.454. Hand 5: EV 145.85 / median 115.4 / 7-chest 44.5% / rainbow 0.597. Hand 6: EV 178.97 / median 155.2 / 7-chest 50.4% / rainbow 0.775.
Green → blue → purple → orange → red → rainbow bands across 24 rows.
Do not greedily complete 7 chests before reaching red/rainbow.
Movement before red/rainbow bands is premium because it preserves access to the payoff zone.
This route has lower 7-chest completion but the best tested EV.
Upgrade cards highest-tier-prioritize, then randomly pick inside that tier.
cards.useWhen: Use as the default Board 3 EV route, especially when you can afford a higher ceiling / lower completion style.
cards.avoidWhen: Avoid if your only goal is guaranteed 7-chest completion; use Red+ Softer Fill or Completion Safe instead.

walkthrough.heading: Early bands
steps: Skip green/blue unless forced.
Avoid filling chest slots before red/rainbow access.
Prioritize movement and forward progress.

walkthrough.heading: Middle bands
steps: Purple is mostly filler, not payoff.
Orange is acceptable if you need bodies, but preserve slots when red/rainbow access looks realistic.
Do not over-complete too early.

walkthrough.heading: Late bands
steps: Prioritize red and rainbow chests.
Use upgrade cards on future high-band targets or owned high-value chests.
This is where the board’s EV comes from.

perStartingHand.note: Board 3 is highly hand-size sensitive; extra cards greatly improve access to late red/rainbow bands.

cards.title: Special 3: Red+ Dynamic
cards.cardSummary: Balanced Board 3 route. Preserves slots for red/rainbow but fills 7 chests more often than the high-ceiling route.
cards.profile: Use this when you want most of the Board 3 EV without accepting as many incomplete runs. It is less greedy than Rainbow/Red High Ceiling and completed 7 chests much more often in testing.
notes: Lower ceiling than High Ceiling, but much better 7-chest completion.
Balanced EV route also scales strongly with starting hand size.
Hand 4: EV 118.16 / median 82.0 / 7-chest 69.3% / rainbow 0.384. Hand 5: EV 138.94 / median 97.2 / 7-chest 73.6% / rainbow 0.485. Hand 6: EV 166.73 / median 126.2 / 7-chest 74.4% / rainbow 0.652.
Strong default if the player dislikes incomplete boards.
Prefer red+, allow orange/purple filler dynamically.
cards.useWhen: Use when you want a balanced mix of EV and 7-chest completion.
cards.avoidWhen: Avoid if maximizing average value is the only goal.

perStartingHand.note: Balanced EV route also scales strongly with starting hand size.

cards.title: Special 3: Red+ Softer Fill
cards.cardSummary: Completion-leaning Board 3 route. Still protects slots, but allows filler sooner.
cards.profile: Use this when you want a smoother route with high 7-chest completion while still avoiding the worst mistake: filling too early with low bands.
notes: Softer Fill keeps completion high while still scaling with hand size.
Hand 4: EV 117.31 / median 80.8 / 7-chest 95.2% / rainbow 0.358. Hand 5: EV 136.95 / median 96.4 / 7-chest 93.2% / rainbow 0.460. Hand 6: EV 159.71 / median 119.8 / 7-chest 90.1% / rainbow 0.604.
Lower EV than High Ceiling and Red+ Dynamic.
Preserve slots, but allow filler sooner.
cards.useWhen: Use when completion consistency matters more than peak EV.
cards.avoidWhen: Avoid when chasing maximum expected value.

perStartingHand.note: Softer Fill keeps completion high while still scaling with hand size.

cards.title: Special 3: Completion Safe
cards.cardSummary: Low-risk Board 3 route. Completes 7 chests very often, but gives up a lot of ceiling.
cards.profile: This is the safer route if you hate incomplete boards. It is not the EV winner because it accepts too many mid/low-value slots.
notes: Highest completion style among shipped Board 3 routes.
Completion Safe scales less explosively because it takes more filler.
Hand 4: EV 99.09 / median 68.2 / 7-chest 97.7% / rainbow 0.216. Hand 5: EV 112.97 / median 82.0 / 7-chest 96.6% / rainbow 0.284. Hand 6: EV 122.39 / median 94.0 / 7-chest 96.3% / rainbow 0.303.
Much lower EV than slot-preservation routes.
For conservative routing or learning the board.
cards.useWhen: Use when 7-chest completion matters more than maximizing value.
cards.avoidWhen: Avoid when trying to maximize expected value.

perStartingHand.note: Completion Safe scales less explosively because it takes more filler.
